metadata.name: WordPress Core Knowledge - Roles & Capabilities
metadata.description: Tests knowledge of WordPress roles and capabilities

tests.prompt: What capability is required by default to install plugins in WordPress?

choices.text: manage_options
install_plugins
activate_plugins
edit_plugins

tests.explanation: The 'install_plugins' capability controls who can install new plugins. It is distinct from activate_plugins (activating/deactivating) and update_plugins (updating existing plugins).

tests.prompt: How many default roles does WordPress ship with?
tests.explanation: WordPress ships with 5 default roles: Administrator, Editor, Author, Contributor, and Subscriber.

tests.prompt: Which capability does a Contributor lack that prevents them from making their posts visible to the public?
tests.explanation: Contributors can edit_posts and delete_posts (their own drafts), but cannot publish_posts. They also lack upload_files and edit_published_posts. Their posts must be published by an Editor or Administrator.

tests.prompt: Which is the lowest role that has the upload_files capability by default?

choices.text: Subscriber
Contributor
Author
Editor

tests.explanation: The upload_files capability is granted to Author, Editor, and Administrator. Contributors and Subscribers cannot upload files by default.

tests.prompt: What is the difference between primitive capabilities and meta capabilities in WordPress?

choices.text: Primitive caps are stored in roles; meta caps are context-dependent and mapped to primitives via map_meta_cap()
Primitive caps apply to posts; meta caps apply to users
Primitive caps are built-in; meta caps are created by plugins
Primitive caps are checked server-side; meta caps are checked client-side

tests.explanation: Primitive capabilities (like edit_posts, edit_others_posts) are stored directly in role definitions. Meta capabilities (like edit_post, delete_post) are abstract/contextual and require an object ID — map_meta_cap() translates them into the required primitive capabilities based on context.

tests.prompt: When map_meta_cap() processes 'edit_user' and the user is editing their own profile, what capabilities are required?

choices.text: edit_users
No additional capabilities — any logged-in user can edit their own profile
manage_options
read and edit_posts

tests.explanation: When $user_id === $args[0] (editing yourself), map_meta_cap() returns an empty $caps array, meaning no special capability is needed. Any authenticated user can edit their own profile.

tests.prompt: What happens when map_meta_cap() returns 'do_not_allow' in the required capabilities array?

choices.text: The check still passes for super admins on multisite
The capability check always fails, even for super admins — it is explicitly unset from all users' capabilities
A _doing_it_wrong notice is triggered
The check is deferred to the user_has_cap filter

tests.explanation: do_not_allow is the ultimate denial. In WP_User::has_cap(), it is explicitly unset from $capabilities. Even super admins on multisite fail the check — the code returns false before granting all-caps if do_not_allow is in the required caps.

tests.prompt: When DISALLOW_UNFILTERED_HTML is defined as true, what happens to the 'unfiltered_html' capability?

choices.text: It is removed from the Administrator role only
It is mapped to do_not_allow for ALL users, including administrators and super admins
It only affects non-super-admin users on multisite
It triggers a deprecation warning but still allows the capability

tests.explanation: When DISALLOW_UNFILTERED_HTML is defined and true, map_meta_cap() maps unfiltered_html to do_not_allow for everyone. This is stronger than any role check — even super admins cannot bypass it.

tests.prompt: On a single-site WordPress installation, is_super_admin() returns true based on which capability?
tests.explanation: On single-site, is_super_admin() checks if the user has_cap('delete_users'). Since only Administrators have this capability by default, all Administrators are effectively super admins on single-site.

tests.prompt: What is the default role assigned to new users who register on a WordPress site?
tests.explanation: The default role for new user registrations is Subscriber. This can be changed in Settings > General under 'New User Default Role'.

tests.prompt: Where are WordPress roles and their capabilities stored in the database?

choices.text: In a dedicated wp_roles table
In the wp_options table under the {prefix}user_roles option key
In the wp_usermeta table for each user
In wp-config.php as a defined constant

tests.explanation: Roles are stored as a serialized array in the wp_options table under the key {$table_prefix}user_roles (e.g., wp_user_roles). Each role entry contains a display name and capabilities array.

tests.prompt: Which capability is the primary gatekeeper for the WordPress Settings pages?
tests.explanation: manage_options is the capability that controls access to the Settings admin pages. Only Administrators have this capability by default.

tests.prompt: The 'exist' capability has special behavior in WP_User::has_cap(). What is it?

choices.text: It returns true only for users who exist in the database
It is hardcoded to true for all users, regardless of their roles
It checks if the user session is still active
It is an alias for the 'read' capability

tests.explanation: In WP_User::has_cap(), the 'exist' capability is hardcoded: $capabilities['exist'] = true. This means any capability check for 'exist' always passes, for every user.

tests.prompt: What capabilities does the Subscriber role have by default?

choices.text: read and edit_posts
read only
read, edit_posts, and upload_files
No capabilities at all

tests.explanation: Subscribers only have the 'read' capability (and the legacy level_0). They cannot create, edit, or delete any content.

tests.prompt: The user_has_cap filter allows plugins to dynamically grant capabilities. What arguments does it receive?

choices.text: $allcaps (all user capabilities), $caps (required primitives), $args (cap name, user ID, object ID), $user (WP_User)
$cap (capability name), $user_id, $grant (boolean)
$user (WP_User), $cap (capability name), $result (boolean)
$allcaps (all user capabilities), $cap (single capability name), $user_id

tests.explanation: The user_has_cap filter passes: $allcaps (full capabilities array), $caps (required primitive caps from map_meta_cap), $args (array with original cap, user ID, and optional object ID), and $user (WP_User object since 3.7.0).
